text.title: Async HTTP Clients
text.summary: httpx.AsyncClient singleton pattern, httpx vs aiohttp selection matrix, and mocking with respx.

rule.statement: Use `httpx` for new async HTTP client code. Use `aiohttp` only when HTTP/2 server-side, WebSocket server capabilities, or maximum-throughput scraping scenarios are needed.
rule.rationale: httpx provides sync + async in one library, HTTP/2 client support, full type annotations, and a requests-compatible API. aiohttp is async-only but offers higher throughput in extreme concurrency scenarios (10,000+ simultaneous connections) and WebSocket server support. Most applications need httpx.

rule.statement: Create `httpx.AsyncClient` once per application (not per request). Pass it via dependency injection or module-level singleton. Never instantiate a new client inside a hot path.
rule.rationale: Creating a new `AsyncClient` per request discards the connection pool and TCP connection reuse. Each new client creates a fresh pool, turning every HTTP call into a full TCP handshake + TLS negotiation — 3-10x slower than reusing an established connection.

example.code: async def get_user(user_id: int) -> dict: async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client: # New client per call! resp = await client.get(f"https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}") return resp.json()

example.note: Module-level singleton with lifespan context management
example.code:
import httpx from contextlib import asynccontextmanager from collections.abc import AsyncGenerator
# Module-level singleton (initialized in app lifespan) _http_client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = None
@asynccontextmanager async def lifespan() -> AsyncGenerator[None, None]: global _http_client _http_client = httpx.AsyncClient( timeout=httpx.Timeout(10.0, connect=3.0), limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=100, max_keepalive_connections=20), ) try: yield finally: await _http_client.aclose()
def get_http_client() -> httpx.AsyncClient: assert _http_client is not None, "HTTP client not initialized" return _http_client
# Usage async def get_user(user_id: int) -> dict: client = get_http_client() resp = await client.get(f"https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}") resp.raise_for_status() return resp.json()

rule.statement: Use `respx` to mock httpx calls in tests. Never mock `httpx.AsyncClient.get` directly — respx intercepts at the transport layer and validates that routes are actually called.
rule.rationale: Mocking `client.get` directly bypasses httpx's request-building logic (URL construction, headers, timeouts). respx intercepts at the ASGI transport layer, meaning the full httpx request-building pipeline runs — only the network call is mocked. This catches URL/header bugs that direct patching hides.

example.note: respx mock for httpx in pytest
example.code:
import pytest import respx import httpx
@pytest.mark.asyncio @respx.mock async def test_get_user() -> None: respx.get("https://api.example.com/users/1").mock( return_value=httpx.Response(200, json={"id": 1, "name": "Alice"}) ) user = await get_user(1) assert user["name"] == "Alice"
